_note: Artem PV chronic-failure cold-production drills (Stage 1 of consolidated plan, batch 1). 17 items: tr_artem_b04-b11 (8 translations) + rt_artem_b03-b11 (9 russian_traps). Targets the 12 chronic PVs surfaced by 2026-05-10 stats-review; 8 highest-priority by Freq×failure-rate, plus 1 paired item for the bring-about/bring-in confusion (rt_artem_b06 + rt_artem_b11 form a mirror pair, like the existing rt_artem_b01/b02 look_for/look_after). Regex traps anchored on actual lastWrong from Artem's qStats. All B2 except go-about (C1). Themes: O&G, KPMG, cycling, Bahrain expat, exec-business.

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: pv_ti17 chronic 0/3, lastWrong = 'drill down into'. Artem reaches for the analyst PV when the prep-PV is wanted.
exercises.prompt_ru: Перед встречей с советом я подготовлюсь по последним финансовым показателям.
correct_answers: Before the board meeting I'll read up on the latest financial figures
Before the board meeting I will read up on the latest financial figures
I'll read up on the latest financial figures before the board meeting
Before the board meeting I'm going to read up on the latest financial figures

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Drill down into' = analyse a result you already have, looking deeper. For *preparing* by studying a topic before encountering it: ✓ 'read up on'. Different timing — drill down is post-data analysis; read up on is pre-meeting prep.
'Study' is correct but textbook-formal. The exec-register PV is 'read up on' — what you do over coffee before the board meeting.
✗ 'Read up the figures' — missing the obligatory particle. ✓ 'read up ON the figures'. Without 'on', the phrasal verb is incomplete.
'Go through' = review existing material in order, often together. 'Read up on' = research a topic in advance, alone. Pre-meeting solo prep → read up on.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'll read up on the latest financial figures.' PV is 'read up on' (research in advance). The 'on' is obligatory.

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: get across cluster 3/7 chronic. Board-comms essential but Artem reaches for 'explain' or 'communicate' (calques, less natural).
exercises.prompt_ru: Мне нужно донести эту мысль до правления так, чтобы они поняли срочность.
correct_answers: I need to get this point across to the board so they understand the urgency
I need to get my point across to the board so they understand the urgency
I need to get this across to the board so they understand the urgency
I need to get this point across to the board so they understand how urgent it is

common_errors.feedback: 'Explain to the board' is correct but flat. For making sure they GET it — register-natural PV: 'get this across to the board'. The PV signals successful transmission, not just delivery.
Russian 'донести' tempts 'communicate' — but exec-register English is 'get X across'. 'Communicate' is corporate-speak; 'get across' is what people actually say in a board meeting.
✗ 'Deliver a point' — you deliver speeches and packages, not points. ✓ 'get the point across'.
When using 'get across' with an audience, name the audience: 'get this across TO the board'. Without 'to + audience' the sentence sounds incomplete.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I need to get this point across to the board.' PV is 'get X across (to Y)' — successfully convey. Don't substitute 'explain' or 'communicate'.

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: turn out ¹ chronic 4/9. Frequent in analyses ('turned out to be') but Artem still reaches for 'appeared' or 'was'.
exercises.prompt_ru: Оказалось, что задержка возникла из-за проблемы с поставщиком, а не из-за нашей логистики.
correct_answers: It turned out the delay was due to a supplier issue, not our logistics
It turned out that the delay was due to a supplier issue, not our logistics
The delay turned out to be due to a supplier issue, not our logistics
It turned out the delay came from a supplier issue, not our logistics
As it turned out, the delay was due to a supplier issue, not our logistics

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'It appeared that' = it seemed (uncertainty). Russian 'оказалось' = was revealed to be → ✓ 'it TURNED OUT that'. Different epistemic load: appeared = guess; turned out = confirmed-after-the-fact.
'We found out' = we discovered (focuses on the discoverer). 'It turned out' = it was revealed (focuses on the fact). Russian 'оказалось' is impersonal — closer to 'turned out'.
'It was revealed' is correct but report-formal. Conversational/exec register: 'it turned out'. Save the passive for written communications.
✗ 'turned out due to' — needs a verb between. ✓ 'turned out the delay WAS due to' or 'turned out TO BE due to'. 'Turn out' takes either a 'that'-clause or 'to be + ...'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'It turned out the delay was due to a supplier issue.' PV is 'turn out' (= prove to be / be revealed as). Russian 'оказалось' → 'turned out', not 'appeared'.

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: bring about chronic 1/6, bring in chronic 0/4. Artem confuses the two: lastWrong on pv_c07 = 'bring in' (when target was bring about).
exercises.prompt_ru: Новая ценовая политика приведёт к существенному изменению нашей маржи.
correct_answers: The new pricing policy will bring about a significant change in our margin
The new pricing policy will bring about a significant change to our margin
The new pricing policy will bring about significant change in our margin
The new pricing policy is going to bring about a significant change in our margin

common_errors.pattern_label: bring in (introduce/earn) instead of bring about (cause)
common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Bring in' = introduce something new (a rule, a person, revenue). For *cause* something to happen: ✓ 'bring ABOUT'. The pair: bring IN = introduce; bring ABOUT = cause/result in. Artem-specific trap — pv_c07 lastWrong was 'bring in' here.
'Lead to' is correct and natural — but the target PV here is 'bring about' (more agentive: the policy actively causes the change). Both work; this drill targets 'bring about' specifically.
'Cause a change' is OK but slightly clinical. Exec-natural: 'bring about a change'. The PV softens 'cause' while keeping the agency.
'Result in' is OK but pushes the policy into passive role. 'Bring about' keeps the policy as active cause — better for board narrative.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'The new pricing policy will bring about a significant change in our margin.' PV is 'bring about' (= cause/result in). Don't confuse with 'bring in' (= introduce/earn).

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: get off chronic 1/7, lastWrong on gt03 = 'get out'. Confused with 'get out of' (non-vehicle).
exercises.prompt_ru: Я выйду на следующей остановке — мне нужно зайти в магазин.
correct_answers: I'll get off at the next stop — I need to drop in at the shop
I will get off at the next stop — I need to drop in at the shop
I'll get off at the next stop — I need to pop into the shop
I'm getting off at the next stop — I need to drop in at the shop
I'll get off at the next stop — I need to stop by the shop

common_errors.pattern_label: get out (instead of get off, for bus/train)
common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Get out at the next stop' — wrong PV. The split: get OUT OF = car/taxi (small enclosed); get OFF = bus/train/plane (transport you stand and walk inside). For 'выйду' on a bus → ✓ 'get OFF'. Artem-specific trap — gt03 lastWrong was 'get out' here.
'Exit' is sign-language English, not spoken. ✓ 'get off at the next stop'.
✗ 'Leave at the next stop' — sounds like you're leaving the bus driver. ✓ 'get OFF at the next stop'. The PV is what disambiguates.
'Step off' is OK in narrative writing but unusual in speech. ✓ 'get off' is the standard.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'll get off at the next stop.' Bus/train/plane → 'get OFF'. Car/taxi → 'get OUT OF'. The vehicle decides the particle.

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: get through ¹ chronic 0/9 (got worse from baseline), lastWrong on gt10 = 'get over'. Confused endure-during with recovery-after.
exercises.prompt_ru: Эта неделя была изматывающая, но как-то я пережил её — день за днём.
correct_answers: It was an exhausting week, but somehow I got through it — day by day
It was an exhausting week, but somehow I got through it day by day
This week was exhausting, but somehow I got through it — day by day
It was an exhausting week, but I managed to get through it — day by day

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Got over it' = recovered AFTER (illness, breakup, shock). For ENDURING DURING a difficult period: ✓ 'got THROUGH it'. Time difference: get over happens after; get through happens during. Artem-specific trap — gt10 lastWrong was 'get over' here.
'Survived the week' is OK conversational hyperbole — but the natural PV is 'got through it'. 'Survive' is dramatic; 'get through' is the everyday register.
'Lived through' works but is reserved for major periods (wars, decades). For an exhausting week — 'got through it'.
✗ 'Passed it' — Russian-style. English uses 'got through it' for enduring difficulty.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I got through it day by day.' PV is 'get through' (endure during). Don't confuse with 'get over' (recover after — different timing).

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: cut down on chronic 1/3, tf_21 0/2. Artem under-uses the PV — reaches for 'reduce' or 'decrease consumption'.
exercises.prompt_ru: После проверки кардиолога я постарался сократить кофе на тренировках.
correct_answers: After the cardiologist's check-up I tried to cut down on coffee during training
After my cardiologist's check-up I tried to cut down on coffee during training
After the cardiologist's check I tried to cut down on coffee during training
After seeing the cardiologist I tried to cut down on coffee during training
After the cardiologist's check-up I've tried to cut down on coffee during training

common_errors.feedback: 'Reduce coffee' is correct but clinical. Exec-natural / cycling-club register: 'cut down on coffee'. The PV signals deliberate, sustained cutback — not a one-off measurement reduction.
'Decrease' is what you do to interest rates, not coffee. Habit-cutting: ✓ 'cut down on'.
✗ 'Cut down coffee' — missing the obligatory particle. ✓ 'cut down ON coffee'. Without 'on', the verb sounds transitive ('cut down' = chop down, like a tree).
✓ 'Cut back on' works (~95% interchangeable with 'cut down on'). Both are correct. This drill targets 'cut down on' specifically — practise that one.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I tried to cut down on coffee during training.' PV is 'cut down on' (reduce a habit). The 'on' is obligatory.

exercises.source_observation: 2026-05-10 stats-review: go about chronic 1/4, lastWrong on ga01 = 'To avoid a topic' (selected wrong meaning entirely). Artem misreads the PV.
exercises.prompt_ru: Как ты обычно подходишь к выбору поставщиков на крупных тендерах?
correct_answers: How do you usually go about choosing suppliers on big tenders
How do you usually go about selecting suppliers on big tenders
How do you typically go about choosing suppliers on big tenders
How do you usually go about choosing suppliers on large tenders
How do you usually go about picking suppliers on big tenders

common_errors.feedback: 'How do you approach choosing X' is correct but flat-formal. Exec-natural / process-talk register: 'how do you go about choosing X'. The PV signals interest in the *method*, not just the activity.
✗ 'Go about choose' — needs gerund. ✓ 'go about CHOOSING'. Pattern: go about + Ving.
✗ 'Go around' = move from place to place / spread (a rumour). ✓ 'go ABOUT' = approach a task. Different particle, different meaning.
'How do you do choosing' is unidiomatic. ✓ 'How do you go about choosing'. The PV is what makes the question natural.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'How do you usually go about choosing suppliers on big tenders?' PV is 'go about + Ving' (= approach a task). Asks for *method*, not just activity.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'почитать о теме перед встречей' = read up on (NOT 'study', NOT 'go through', NOT 'drill down into'). Artem reaches for analyst-PVs (drill down) when prep-PV is wanted. Failure mode: confusing pre-meeting prep (read up on) with post-data analysis (drill down into).
exercises.prompt_ru: Я почитаю о клиенте перед звонком.
correct_answers: I'll read up on the client before the call
I will read up on the client before the call
I'm going to read up on the client before the call

common_errors.pattern_label: drill down into (analyst PV, wrong timing)
common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Drill down' = analyse data already in front of you. For *preparing* by researching the client beforehand: ✓ 'read up on the client'. Read up on = pre-meeting; drill down = mid-analysis.
Russian 'почитать' is casual; English 'study' is exam-formal. The natural PV: 'read up on'.
✗ 'Read up the client' — missing 'on'. ✓ 'read up ON the client'. The 'on' is obligatory.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'll read up on the client before the call.' Russian 'почитать о' → 'read up ON'. Don't substitute 'drill down into' (that's for analysing data, not preparing).

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'донести (мысль)' = get across. Artem reaches for 'communicate' or 'explain' (calque-safe but flat). Get across signals successful transmission, not just delivery — and is the exec-natural choice.
exercises.prompt_ru: Не уверен, что мне удалось донести свою мысль.
correct_answers: I'm not sure I got my point across
I am not sure I got my point across
I'm not sure I managed to get my point across
I'm not sure I got the point across

common_errors.feedback: 'Explain my point' = stated it. 'Got my point across' = stated it AND landed. Russian 'донести' has the landing-implied — match it with 'get across'.
'Communicate' is corporate-presentation-deck. 'Get across' is what people say in real conversation. Use the PV.
✗ 'Deliver a point' — collocates poorly. You deliver speeches, packages, and bad news. Points and ideas: ✓ 'get across'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I got my point across.' Russian 'донести' → 'get X across'. Not 'explain' (no landing-implied), not 'communicate' (too formal).

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'оказаться (X)' = turn out (to be X). Artem substitutes 'appeared' (Russian-leaning, weaker epistemic load) or 'was' (drops the after-the-fact framing). Turn out implies confirmation-after-the-fact.
exercises.prompt_ru: Этот контракт оказался выгоднее, чем мы ожидали.
correct_answers: This contract turned out to be more profitable than we expected
The contract turned out to be more profitable than we expected
This contract turned out more profitable than we expected
It turned out the contract was more profitable than we expected

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'The contract appeared more profitable' = it seemed (uncertain). Russian 'оказаться' = was confirmed-after-the-fact → ✓ 'turned out'. Different epistemic strength: appeared = guess; turned out = settled fact.
'The contract was more profitable' is grammatical but loses the *revelation* — the fact that we found out only in retrospect. Russian 'оказался' carries that surprise; English: 'turned out to be'.
'Happened to be' = by chance / coincidentally. Russian 'оказался' here = was revealed-after-analysis. 'Turned out to be' is the right match.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'This contract turned out to be more profitable than we expected.' Russian 'оказался' → 'turned out (to be)'. The PV carries the after-the-fact revelation.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'привести к (изменению)' = bring about (cause). Artem confuses with 'bring in' (introduce). The pair: bring ABOUT = cause; bring IN = introduce. Russian 'привести к' specifically maps to bring about.
exercises.prompt_ru: Реструктуризация привела к серьёзному снижению затрат.
correct_answers: The restructuring brought about a significant reduction in costs
The restructuring has brought about a significant reduction in costs
The restructuring brought about significant cost reduction
The restructuring has brought about significant cost reduction

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Brought in a reduction' — 'bring in' = introduce something new (a person, a rule, revenue). For *cause* a result: ✓ 'bring ABOUT'. Pair trap: bring IN = introduce; bring ABOUT = cause.

common_errors.pattern_label: led to (correct synonym, but target is bring about)
common_errors.feedback: ✓ 'Led to' is correct and natural. This drill targets 'bring about' specifically. Try the PV: 'brought about a significant reduction'.
'Caused a reduction' is correct but accident-tinged ('what caused this?'). For deliberate restructuring outcomes: ✓ 'brought about'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'The restructuring brought about a significant reduction in costs.' Russian 'привести к' → 'bring about'. Don't confuse with 'bring in' (= introduce/earn).

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'выйти из автобуса/поезда' = get OFF (NOT get out of). Vehicle decides the particle: small enclosed (car/taxi) → get out of; transport-you-walk-inside (bus/train/plane) → get off. Artem swaps to 'get out' for buses.
exercises.prompt_ru: Я вышел из метро на станции Манама и пошёл пешком до офиса.
correct_answers: I got off the metro at Manama station and walked to the office
I got off at Manama station and walked to the office
I got off the metro at Manama and walked to the office
I got off the train at Manama station and walked to the office

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Got out of the metro' — wrong particle. Vehicle rule: car/taxi (small enclosed) → get OUT OF; metro/bus/train/plane (transport you walk inside) → get OFF. ✓ 'got OFF the metro'.
'Left the metro' is OK but loses the boundary-crossing meaning. ✓ 'got OFF the metro' is the standard.
'Exited' is bureaucratic. Speech: 'got off'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I got off the metro at Manama station.' Vehicle rule: bus/train/metro/plane → get OFF; car/taxi → get OUT OF.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'пережить (трудный период)' = get through (endure during). Artem swaps to 'get over' (recover after) — wrong timing. Pair distinction: get THROUGH = during the difficulty; get OVER = after it ended.
exercises.prompt_ru: Не знаю как, но я пережил этот квартал.
correct_answers: I don't know how, but I got through this quarter
I don't know how, but I got through the quarter
I'm not sure how, but I got through this quarter
Somehow I got through this quarter

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Got over the quarter' — 'get over' = recovered AFTER (illness, loss, shock). Russian 'пережить' here = endured DURING → ✓ 'got THROUGH'. Time difference: through = during; over = after. Artem-specific trap — gt10 lastWrong was 'get over' here.
'Survived the quarter' is OK conversational hyperbole — but the PV is 'got through it'. Save 'survive' for catastrophes.
✗ 'Passed the quarter' = Russian-style. English: 'got through it'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I got through this quarter.' Russian 'пережить' (endure) → 'get THROUGH' (during). Different from 'get OVER' (after).

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'сократить (что-то в рационе/расходах)' = cut down on. Artem reaches for 'reduce' or 'decrease' (clinical). The 'on' is obligatory and frequently dropped.
correct_answers: I've cut down on sugar after the test results
I cut down on sugar after the test results
I have cut down on sugar after the test results
I've cut down on sugar after the blood tests
I cut down on sugar after the blood tests

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Cut down sugar' — missing 'on'. ✓ 'cut down ON sugar'. Without 'on' the verb sounds like chopping (cut down a tree). The 'on' is obligatory before the consumed thing.
'Reduce sugar' is clinical (like a prescription). Habit-cutting register: 'cut down on sugar'.
✓ 'Cut back on' is correct (~95% interchangeable). This drill targets 'cut down on'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I've cut down on sugar.' Russian 'сократить (в рационе)' → 'cut down ON'. The 'on' is obligatory.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'подходить к (задаче/процессу)' = go about + Ving. Artem misreads the PV (selected 'avoid a topic' as the meaning on ga01). Pattern: go about = approach a task; needs gerund object.
exercises.prompt_ru: Я не уверен, как подходить к этому переговорному процессу.
correct_answers: I'm not sure how to go about this negotiation
I am not sure how to go about this negotiation
I'm not sure how to go about negotiating this
I'm not sure how to go about handling this negotiation
I'm not sure how to go about approaching this negotiation

common_errors.feedback: 'How to approach' is correct but flat. Process-talk register: 'how to go about this negotiation'. The PV invites discussion of *method*.
✗ 'Go about handle' — needs gerund. ✓ 'go about HANDLING' / 'go about NEGOTIATING'. Pattern: go about + Ving (or + noun).
✗ 'Go around' = avoid / move from place to place. ✓ 'go ABOUT' = approach methodically.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm not sure how to go about this negotiation.' Russian 'подходить к (процессу)' → 'go about'. Followed by gerund or noun. Don't substitute 'approach'.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'привлечь / ввести / привнести' = bring in (introduce or earn). Artem confuses with 'bring about' (cause). Pair direction here: bring IN = introduce/recruit/earn revenue. Mirror of rt_artem_b05b.
exercises.prompt_ru: Мы хотим привлечь нового подрядчика для второго этапа.
correct_answers: We want to bring in a new contractor for the second phase
We'd like to bring in a new contractor for the second phase
We want to bring in a new contractor for phase two
We're looking to bring in a new contractor for the second phase

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Bring about a new contractor' — 'bring about' = cause/result in (an outcome). For *introduce / recruit*: ✓ 'bring IN'. Pair trap mirror: bring ABOUT = cause; bring IN = introduce. Russian 'привлечь' here = recruit → bring in.
'Attract a contractor' is OK in marketing-speak. For *deliberately bringing one onto the project*: ✓ 'bring in a contractor'.
'Hire' is correct and direct. 'Bring in' is preferred when you want to signal a new addition to an existing team — softer than 'hire'. Both work; this drill targets 'bring in'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'We want to bring in a new contractor for the second phase.' Russian 'привлечь / ввести' → 'bring IN'. Different from 'bring about' (= cause an outcome).
